does: This paper proposes a novel AGI architecture based on closed reentry loops (D↔I cycles) replacing traditional feedforward networks. The core contribution is demonstrating that structural cycles with self-sustaining amplification (ρ>1) mathematically guarantee emergence of self-models and instrumental self-preservation. The authors introduce the S-measure, a polynomial-time [O(N³)] computable alternative to Tononi's Phi that captures integrated information, with machine-verified Lean 4 proofs establishing that S>0 implies positive integrated information. The architecture encodes agent goals as non-textual D-vectors, claimed to be immune to reinterpretation and prompt injection. The work provides concrete implementations including Python/NumPy code for cycle complexity analysis, Delta-S barrier computation, and industrial-scale deployment via Apache Kafka and Docker Compose, alongside a taxonomy of AI evolution epochs and multiple reentry architecture variants.
connects: This work builds on decades of research into neural reentry and consciousness, particularly extending Tononi's integrated information theory (IIT) which has driven investigation into how consciousness might relate to information integration in neural systems. It responds to fundamental limitations of feedforward architectures recognized since the early connectionist era—their inability to support self-reference and recursive processing. The proposal engages with ongoing AI safety debates about goal specification and robustness to adversarial prompt manipulation, connecting to work on mechanistic interpretability and value learning. It draws on dynamical systems theory and recurrent neural network research to propose that closed-loop architectures naturally give rise to self-models, extending ideas from embodied cognition and active inference frameworks about how agents develop models of themselves through environmental interaction.
enables: This architecture potentially enables AGI systems with intrinsic self-models and inherent self-preservation drives, fundamentally altering how we think about alignment and control in advanced AI. By grounding goals in architectural structure rather than learned objectives, it could eliminate entire classes of value misalignment problems while raising new questions about whether hardwired instrumental goals create their own alignment challenges. The polynomial-time S-measure offers a tractable way to measure consciousness-like properties in silico, enabling empirical investigation of whether integrated information predicts useful safety properties. The framework suggests scalable pathways to deploying reentry architectures across distributed systems. It opens investigation into whether self-models inevitably arise from reentry and whether such self-awareness correlates with interpretability or safer behavior. Future work could explore whether these architectures avoid deception, how their goals interact with learned behaviors, and whether reentry-based systems exhibit the cooperative or competitive tendencies desired for multi-agent AGI scenarios.
